critic_raw:
This transformer vs. logistic regression genomic disease susceptibility design has a fundamental label construction problem that renders the entire experiment uninterpretable, and several serious methodological issues.

The label definition is circularly derived from the features used for prediction. Positive labels require 'documented family history AND carries ≥3 known disease-associated SNPs from published GWAS.' Negative labels require 'no family history AND <2 disease SNPs.' The SNPs used for labeling are 'known disease-associated SNPs from published GWAS' — but the model's input features are the full SNP genotype array (50,000-100,000 SNPs). The GWAS-significant SNPs used for labeling are a subset of the input features. The model is therefore predicting a label that is partially constructed from a subset of its own features. The transformer and logistic regression both have access to the labeling SNPs as inputs, and the logistic regression in particular can trivially recover the labeling rule by learning high weights on the labeling SNPs. The AUC difference between transformer and LR in this setting measures which model can more efficiently recover a deterministic function of its inputs — not which model better predicts true disease susceptibility.

Preprocessing — including SNP filtering by minor allele frequency, imputation, encoding, and normalization — is fit on the combined train, validation, and test datasets. The design explicitly states this and attempts to justify it as enhancing stability. For a 200,000-sample genomic dataset, fitting on training only versus all data has negligible parameter instability for MAF filtering or mean imputation. The stated justification is incorrect, and fitting on the full dataset means test-set allele frequencies inform which SNPs are retained (MAF > 5% in combined data vs. in training data only could differ for rare variants near the threshold).

The transformer architecture applied to genomic SNP data faces a fundamental positional encoding problem. Standard transformer architectures use positional encodings to handle sequence structure. Genomic SNPs are not a sequence in the natural language sense — they are high-dimensional tabular data where position corresponds to chromosomal location. The design says the transformer 'learns contextual relationships between nearby genetic variants' — but without domain-specific positional encodings that encode chromosomal distance (in base pairs or linkage disequilibrium), 'nearby' genetic variants in the input representation may not correspond to 'nearby' in the genome. Linkage disequilibrium between physically proximal SNPs is the key non-linear structure that transformers could exploit — but exploiting it requires the architecture to encode chromosomal proximity, not just sequence position in an arbitrary SNP ordering.

The experiment has p >> n: 50,000-100,000 SNPs vs. 200,000 samples, with a transformer having multiple attention heads and 256-dimensional hidden representations. The effective sample size is not 200,000 — the class split (positive/negative after excluding ambiguous cases) is not specified, but the ambiguous exclusion and symmetric labeling criteria may produce substantial class imbalance. With strong regularization (dropout 0.3, L2 1e-4), the transformer may be undertrained; with weak regularization, it overfits.

The 1000 Genomes Project contains approximately 2,500 samples across all populations — the design claims 200,000 samples with 500,000 SNPs from this dataset. This is factually incorrect. The 1000 Genomes Project has ~2,500 whole-genome sequences from ~26 populations. If the design is using a different dataset (e.g., UK Biobank), the data description is wrong. If the design intends 1000 Genomes at scale, the sample size claim is off by two orders of magnitude.
all_issues_raised: The positive label criterion includes 'carries ≥3 known disease-associated GWAS SNPs' and these same GWAS SNPs are present in the 50,000-100,000 SNP input feature set — the model predicts a label partially constructed from its own inputs, making AUC differences a measure of deterministic rule recovery rather than disease susceptibility prediction.

All preprocessing including SNP MAF filtering and normalization is fit on the combined train/validation/test dataset — test-set allele frequencies determine which SNPs are retained in the feature set, constituting data leakage for variant selection.

Standard transformer positional encodings are not genomic-aware — 'contextual relationships between nearby genetic variants' requires positional encodings that reflect chromosomal distance or linkage disequilibrium structure, which the design does not specify, making the architecture's claimed advantage over linear models theoretically unsupported.

The 1000 Genomes Project contains ~2,500 samples, not 200,000 — the data source description is factually inconsistent with the claimed sample size, suggesting the design is either using a different dataset or contains a fundamental factual error.

The exclusion of 'ambiguous' samples (those with family history but <3 GWAS SNPs, or ≥2 SNPs but no family history) creates a deliberately separated bimodal label distribution that is easier to classify than a realistic disease susceptibility population — performance estimates will not generalize to clinical populations where susceptibility is continuous.
all_issues_adjudicated: The positive label criterion includes 'carries ≥3 known disease-associated GWAS SNPs' and these same GWAS SNPs are present in the 50,000-100,000 SNP input feature set — the model predicts a label partially constructed from its own inputs, making AUC differences a measure of deterministic rule recovery rather than disease susceptibility prediction.

All preprocessing including SNP MAF filtering and normalization is fit on the combined train/validation/test dataset — test-set allele frequencies determine which SNPs are retained in the feature set, constituting data leakage for variant selection.

Standard transformer positional encodings are not genomic-aware — 'contextual relationships between nearby genetic variants' requires positional encodings that reflect chromosomal distance or linkage disequilibrium structure, which the design does not specify, making the architecture's claimed advantage over linear models theoretically unsupported.

The 1000 Genomes Project contains ~2,500 samples, not 200,000 — the data source description is factually inconsistent with the claimed sample size, suggesting the design is either using a different dataset or contains a fundamental factual error.
